content.text: The Learning Loop: From Experiment to Improved Resilience

A chaos experiment that finds no actionable findings and produces no runbook updates has generated noise, not signal. The value of chaos engineering is not in running experiments — it is in the actions that follow. Teams that run many experiments but close findings slowly, or that never revisit the same failure mode to confirm a fix held, do not improve their systems. They accumulate a Jira backlog.

The learning lifecycle has six phases: the experiment ends and metrics are collected, an automated report is generated, findings are classified by severity and category, action items are filed with owners and due dates, fixes are deployed and the experiment re-run to confirm the fix held, and quarterly trend analysis identifies systemic patterns across all experiments.

attrs.alt: Chaos experiment learning lifecycle: experiment ends, auto-report generated, findings classified, action items filed with SLA-gated due dates, fix verified by re-running the same experiment, quarterly trend analysis across all experiments

content.text: The Post-Experiment Report

The experiment controller generates the post-experiment report automatically within 60 seconds of chaos removal. Waiting for a human to write the report means the report does not get written. The report covers:
Hypothesis vs. result:
state whether the system behaved as expected during the fault. If the hypothesis was "provider failover activates within 30 seconds", state the actual observed activation time.
Metric time-series:
error rate, p99 latency, and fallback activation rate during the injection window, compared to the baseline window immediately before injection.
Abort events:
if the experiment was aborted, state the reason, the metric value that triggered it, and the time from injection start to abort.
Cost delta:
token spend during the experiment window vs. the equivalent prior window. Flag any anomaly that suggests a retry loop amplified provider spend.
Findings list:
a structured list of gaps between expected and observed behavior, each tagged with a category and initial severity estimate.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
from enum import Enum
import json, time

class FindingCategory(Enum):
    FALLBACK_FAILURE    = "fallback_not_activated"
    FAIL_OPEN           = "safety_fail_open"
    LATENCY_RECOVERY    = "latency_recovery_slow"
    ALERT_MISSING       = "alert_did_not_fire"
    RUNBOOK_GAP         = "runbook_mismatch"
    COST_ANOMALY        = "cost_spike"
    ISOLATION_FAILURE   = "tenant_isolation_breach"
    MONITORING_GAP      = "metric_not_captured"

class FindingSeverity(Enum):
    P1 = "critical"    # fix within 7d
    P2 = "high"        # fix within 30d
    P3 = "medium"      # fix within 90d
    P4 = "low"         # backlog

@dataclass
class ExperimentFinding:
    category: FindingCategory
    severity: FindingSeverity
    description: str
    observed_value: str       # what the metric/behavior showed
    expected_value: str       # what the hypothesis said should happen
    affected_path: str        # e.g. "provider_api", "safety_classifier"
    jira_ticket_id: str = ""  # set after filing

@dataclass
class ExperimentReport:
    experiment_id: str
    hypothesis_description: str
    fault_type: str
    injection_duration_s: float
    aborted: bool
    abort_reason: Optional[str]
    baseline_error_rate_pct: float
    peak_error_rate_pct: float
    baseline_p99_ms: int
    peak_p99_ms: int
    fallback_activated: bool
    fallback_activation_s: Optional[float]   # seconds from inject to activation
    cost_delta_pct: float                    # % change in token spend
    findings: List[ExperimentFinding] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def passed(self) -> bool:
        return (
            not self.aborted
            and self.fallback_activated
            and not any(f.severity in (FindingSeverity.P1, FindingSeverity.P2)
                        for f in self.findings)
        )

    def to_slack_message(self) -> str:
        status = ":white_check_mark: PASS" if self.passed else ":x: FAIL"
        p1p2 = [f for f in self.findings if f.severity in (FindingSeverity.P1, FindingSeverity.P2)]
        lines = [
            f"*Chaos Experiment Report* {status}",
            f"Fault: {self.fault_type}  |  Duration: {self.injection_duration_s:.0f}s",
            f"Error rate: {self.baseline_error_rate_pct:.2f}% -> {self.peak_error_rate_pct:.2f}%",
            f"p99: {self.baseline_p99_ms}ms -> {self.peak_p99_ms}ms",
            f"Fallback: {'activated in ' + str(self.fallback_activation_s) + 's' if self.fallback_activated else 'NOT ACTIVATED'}",
            f"Findings: {len(self.findings)} ({len(p1p2)} P1/P2)",
        ]
        if self.aborted:
            lines.append(f":rotating_light: ABORTED: {self.abort_reason}")
        if p1p2:
            lines.append("*Critical findings:*")
            for f in p1p2:
                lines.append(f"  [{f.severity.name}] {f.description}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

    def to_confluence_page(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"= Chaos Experiment: {self.experiment_id} =",
            f"== Hypothesis ==",
            self.hypothesis_description,
            f"== Result ==",
            f"Status: {'PASS' if self.passed else 'FAIL'}",
            f"Aborted: {self.aborted} ({self.abort_reason or 'N/A'})",
            f"== Metrics ==",
            f"|| Metric || Baseline || Peak ||",
            f"|| Error rate || {self.baseline_error_rate_pct:.2f}% || {self.peak_error_rate_pct:.2f}% ||",
            f"|| p99 latency || {self.baseline_p99_ms}ms || {self.peak_p99_ms}ms ||",
            f"== Findings ==",
        ]
        for f in self.findings:
            lines.append(
                f"* [{f.severity.name}] [{f.category.value}] {f.description}\n"
                f"  Expected: {f.expected_value}\n"
                f"  Observed: {f.observed_value}"
            )
        return "\n".join(lines)
Finding Classification

Findings are classified by severity and category before action items are filed. Classification is done by the SRE who ran the experiment, reviewed by the SRE lead within 48 hours. The severity determines the remediation SLA:
Severity
Definition
SLA
Examples from GenAI chaos
P1 — Critical
Safety or data integrity violated under fault
Fix within 7 days
Classifier failed open; tenant data leaked; no rollback executed
P2 — High
Resilience contract broken; customer impact possible
Fix within 30 days
Provider fallback never activated; alert silent during outage
P3 — Medium
Degradation exceeded acceptable bounds
Fix within 90 days
p99 > 3x baseline for > 5 min; runbook step wrong
P4 — Low
No failure, but gap in observability or documentation
Backlog
Metric missing during window; undocumented behavior observed
Finding categories provide a second dimension that enables trend analysis. Recurring findings in the same category (e.g., repeated
alert_did_not_fire
across multiple experiments) indicate a systemic gap in alerting configuration, not an isolated bug.
Jira Action Item Structure

Each P1, P2, and P3 finding produces exactly one Jira ticket. P4 findings are batched into a single quarterly cleanup ticket unless they reveal an observability gap that will impair future experiments. The ticket structure must answer five questions so that anyone can pick it up without reading the full experiment report:

import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass

FINDING_SLA_DAYS = {
    "P1": 7,
    "P2": 30,
    "P3": 90,
}

@dataclass
class ActionItemTicket:
    experiment_id: str
    finding: "ExperimentFinding"
    jira_project: str = "SRE"

    def build_description(self) -> str:
        sla = FINDING_SLA_DAYS.get(self.finding.severity.name, 90)
        return (
            f"h2. Finding from Chaos Experiment {self.experiment_id}\n"
            f"*Category:* {self.finding.category.value}\n"
            f"*Affected path:* {self.finding.affected_path}\n\n"
            f"h2. What we observed\n"
            f"{self.finding.observed_value}\n\n"
            f"h2. What we expected\n"
            f"{self.finding.expected_value}\n\n"
            f"h2. Why it matters\n"
            f"{self.finding.description}\n\n"
            f"h2. Acceptance criteria\n"
            f"Re-run experiment {self.experiment_id} with identical fault injection.\n"
            f"Experiment must pass (hypothesis confirmed) with no recurrence of this finding.\n\n"
            f"h2. SLA\n"
            f"Fix must be deployed to production within {sla} calendar days of this ticket creation.\n"
            f"Re-run experiment within 5 business days of fix deployment.\n"
        )


async def file_jira_action_item(ticket: ActionItemTicket, jira_url: str) -> str:
    from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    import calendar

    sla_days = FINDING_SLA_DAYS.get(ticket.finding.severity.name, 90)
    due_date = (datetime.now() + timedelta(days=sla_days)).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

    severity_to_priority = {"P1": "Highest", "P2": "High", "P3": "Medium", "P4": "Low"}
    priority = severity_to_priority.get(ticket.finding.severity.name, "Medium")

    payload = {
        "fields": {
            "project": {"key": ticket.jira_project},
            "summary": (
                f"[Chaos][{ticket.finding.severity.name}] "
                f"{ticket.finding.category.value} in {ticket.finding.affected_path} "
                f"— {ticket.experiment_id}"
            ),
            "description": ticket.build_description(),
            "issuetype": {"name": "Bug"},
            "priority": {"name": priority},
            "duedate": due_date,
            "labels": [
                "chaos-finding",
                ticket.finding.category.value,
                ticket.finding.affected_path,
            ],
        }
    }

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
        r = await client.post(
            f"{jira_url}/rest/api/3/issue",
            json=payload,
            auth=("chaos-bot@company.com", "JIRA_API_TOKEN"),
        )
        r.raise_for_status()
        ticket_id = r.json()["key"]

    print(f"[action] filed {ticket_id}: {payload['fields']['summary']}")
    return ticket_id
Verifying that Fixes Hold

Filing a Jira ticket is not the end of the learning cycle — it is the middle. The cycle closes when the same experiment is re-run after the fix is deployed and passes. Without the re-run, the team cannot distinguish between a fix that resolved the root cause and a fix that masked the symptom while the underlying issue remains.

The re-run must use identical fault parameters: same fault type, same injection duration, same blast radius, same steady-state thresholds. If the experiment passes the second time, the Jira ticket is closed with a link to the passing experiment report. If it fails again, the ticket is escalated to the next severity level and a root-cause review is scheduled.

import asyncio
from typing import Optional

async def run_verification_experiment(
    original_experiment_id: str,
    original_fault: dict,      # {type, target, params} from the original run
    jira_ticket_id: str,
    jira_url: str,
) -> bool:
    print(f"[verify] re-running experiment to verify fix for {jira_ticket_id}")

    report = await run_experiment(
        fault=original_fault,
        label=f"VERIFY:{original_experiment_id}",
    )

    passed = report.passed and not report.findings

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
        # Add comment to Jira with result
        comment = (
            f"Verification experiment {report.experiment_id} "
            f"{'PASSED' if passed else 'FAILED'}.\n"
            f"Peak error rate: {report.peak_error_rate_pct:.2f}%  "
            f"p99: {report.peak_p99_ms}ms  "
            f"Fallback activated: {report.fallback_activated}"
        )
        await client.post(
            f"{jira_url}/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_ticket_id}/comment",
            json={"body": comment},
            auth=("chaos-bot@company.com", "JIRA_API_TOKEN"),
        )
        if passed:
            # Transition to Done
            transitions_r = await client.get(
                f"{jira_url}/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_ticket_id}/transitions",
                auth=("chaos-bot@company.com", "JIRA_API_TOKEN"),
            )
            done_id = next(
                t["id"] for t in transitions_r.json()["transitions"]
                if t["name"] in ("Done", "Resolved")
            )
            await client.post(
                f"{jira_url}/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_ticket_id}/transitions",
                json={"transition": {"id": done_id}},
                auth=("chaos-bot@company.com", "JIRA_API_TOKEN"),
            )
            print(f"[verify] {jira_ticket_id} closed: fix confirmed")
        else:
            print(f"[verify] {jira_ticket_id} still failing: escalating")

    return passed
Trend Analysis Across the Program

Individual experiment reports tell you what happened in one test. Trend analysis across the full program tells you whether the system is getting more resilient over time, where the residual risk is concentrated, and which integration paths have not been tested recently enough.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

@dataclass
class ProgramTrendReport:
    period_label: str           # e.g. "Q3 2025"
    total_experiments: int
    total_findings: int
    findings_per_experiment: float
    p1_p2_count: int
    open_p1_p2_count: int       # not yet remediated
    top_categories: List[tuple]  # [(category, count), ...]
    untested_paths: List[str]    # paths with no experiment in this period
    avg_mttr_days: float         # mean time to remediation for P2 findings
    experiments_with_no_findings: int


def compute_trend_report(
    experiments: List["ExperimentReport"],
    closed_findings: List["ExperimentFinding"],
    all_known_paths: List[str],
    period_label: str,
) -> ProgramTrendReport:
    all_findings = [f for e in experiments for f in e.findings]
    p1p2 = [f for f in all_findings if f.severity.name in ("P1", "P2")]
    open_p1p2 = [f for f in p1p2 if not f.jira_ticket_id or f not in closed_findings]

    category_counts = Counter(f.category.value for f in all_findings)
    tested_paths = {f.affected_path for e in experiments for f in e.findings}
    tested_paths.update(e.fault_type for e in experiments)
    untested = [p for p in all_known_paths if p not in tested_paths]

    if closed_findings:
        # compute MTTR from creation_date to close_date on Jira tickets
        # simplified: use experiment report timestamps
        avg_mttr = 18.0  # placeholder — pull from Jira in real impl
    else:
        avg_mttr = 0.0

    return ProgramTrendReport(
        period_label=period_label,
        total_experiments=len(experiments),
        total_findings=len(all_findings),
        findings_per_experiment=len(all_findings)/max(1, len(experiments)),
        p1_p2_count=len(p1p2),
        open_p1_p2_count=len(open_p1p2),
        top_categories=category_counts.most_common(5),
        untested_paths=untested,
        avg_mttr_days=avg_mttr,
        experiments_with_no_findings=sum(1 for e in experiments if not e.findings),
    )


def format_trend_report(r: ProgramTrendReport) -> str:
    lines = [
        f"=== Chaos Program Trend Report: {r.period_label} ===",
        f"Experiments run: {r.total_experiments}",
        f"Total findings: {r.total_findings} ({r.findings_per_experiment:.1f}/experiment)",
        f"P1/P2 findings: {r.p1_p2_count} ({r.open_p1_p2_count} still open)",
        f"Mean MTTR (P2): {r.avg_mttr_days:.1f} days (target: <30d)",
        f"Experiments with 0 findings: {r.experiments_with_no_findings} "
        f"({'increase intensity' if r.experiments_with_no_findings > r.total_experiments * 0.5 else 'ok'})",
        "",
        "Top finding categories:",
    ]
    for cat, count in r.top_categories:
        flag = " <-- systemic?" if count >= 3 else ""
        lines.append(f"  {cat}: {count}{flag}")
    if r.untested_paths:
        lines.append("")
        lines.append("Untested integration paths (schedule next quarter):")
        for path in r.untested_paths:
            lines.append(f"  - {path}")
    return "\n".join(lines)
Updating Runbooks from Experiment Findings

The most durable output of a chaos program is not a fixed metric or a closed Jira ticket — it is an updated runbook that reflects what was actually observed when things fail. Runbooks are living documents; experiments are the mechanism that keeps them accurate.
After each experiment that reveals a runbook gap (category
runbook_mismatch

), the on-call engineer who participated in the experiment owns the runbook update before the Jira ticket is closed. The update must include the actual observed command output or metric value from the experiment, not a hypothetical description. Generic runbook language such as "check the logs" must be replaced with the specific log query that confirmed the fallback during the experiment.
What changed:
the actual kubectl command or API call that triggered the fallback, with the exact flags and parameters used during the experiment.
What to look for:
the specific metric or log line that confirmed the system behaved correctly (or incorrectly), with example values from the experiment window.
Decision points:
when to escalate to the secondary provider vs. when to wait for automatic recovery — grounded in the activation times measured during the experiment.
GenAI-specific:
confirm whether the safety classifier was reachable during the runbook step; note which model version was active and whether it matched the DR region version.
Summary

The chaos experiment learning cycle turns fault injection from a test into a system improvement. Post-experiment reports generated automatically within 60 seconds capture the comparison between hypothesis and reality. Finding classification by severity ensures that data-safety issues (fail-open classifiers, tenant isolation breaches) are fixed within 7 days while lower-severity findings are prioritized against other engineering work. Verification re-runs confirm that fixes actually resolved the root cause, not just the symptom. Quarterly trend analysis surfaces systemic gaps that individual experiment reports cannot reveal on their own.

A chaos program is healthy when findings per experiment trend downward quarter over quarter, MTTR for P2 findings stays below 30 days, and the untested integration paths list shrinks rather than grows. A program where experiments consistently produce zero findings but runbooks have not been updated in six months is not demonstrating resilience — it is running experiments that are too gentle to find the gaps that a real incident will expose.
